paper_title: Quickest Detection of Hallucination Onset: Delay Bounds and Learned CUSUM Statistics

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Token-level hallucination detectors are evaluated as classifiers, by AUC over all tokens, yet a streaming monitor is judged by its reaction time: the number of tokens that pass between the onset of a hallucination and the alarm. We formulate hallucination onset detection as a quickest change detection problem. A first-order Markov model of the latent faithful/hallucinated state, validated on RAGTruth, places the task inside classical change-point theory and yields Lorden's lower bound on detection delay: about 1.3 tokens at a false-alarm rate of 0.01. We then show that a causal recurrent labeler acts as a CUSUM with a learned increment. Among the onsets it catches it detects in 11-13 tokens, against 31 for a linear per-token baseline, though at this false-alarm budget every detector catches under a third of onsets and the recall-honest delay is 56-66 tokens: low-false-alarm onset detection is hard. A controlled decomposition attributes the speed advantage mostly to a better per-token score rather than to temporal accumulation. An information-rate optimality theorem of Donsker-Varadhan type explains the remaining order-of-magnitude gap: the learned score realizes only 1/4.5 of the divergence the features carry, a deficit that recalibration cannot remove, with the remainder a finite-horizon effect. Classification metrics conceal this delay structure; sequential analysis makes it measurable.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 2 1 ] . [ 2 6 7 4 2 1 . 6 0 6 2 : r Quickest Detection of Hallucination Onset: Delay Bounds and Learned CUSUM Statistics Igor Itkin Independent Researcher ig.itkin@gmail.com Abstract Token-level hallucination detectors are evaluated as classifiers, by AUC over all tokens, yet streaming monitor is judged by its reaction time: the number of tokens that pass between the onset of hallucination and the alarm. We formulate hallucination onset detection as quickest change detection problem. first-order Markov model of the latent faithful/hallucinated state, validated on RAGTruth, places the task inside classical change-point theory and yields Lordens lower bound on detection delay: about 1.3 tokens at false-alarm rate of 0.01. We then show that causal recurrent labeler acts as CUSUM with learned increment. Among the onsets it catches it detects in 1113 tokens, against 31 for linear per-token baseline, though at this false-alarm budget every detector catches under third of onsets and the recall-honest delay is 5666 tokens: low-false-alarm onset detection is hard. controlled decomposition attributes the speed advantage mostly to better per-token score rather than to temporal accumulation. An information-rate optimality theorem of DonskerVaradhan type explains the remaining order-of-magnitude gap: the learned score realizes only 1/4.5 of the divergence the features carry, deficit that recalibration cannot remove, with the remainder finite-horizon effect. Classification metrics conceal this delay structure; sequential analysis makes it measurable.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Put hallucination detector in front of language model that streams tokens to user, and one number decides whether it is useful: once the model starts making things up, how many tokens slip out before the detector raises the alarm? monitor that flags hallucination ten tokens after it began has already let false claim reach the reader. Yet the field measures token-level detectors almost exclusively as classifiers, by area under the ROC curve over all tokens. That score rewards getting the average token right. It says nothing about how quickly detector reacts to the moment that matters, the onset. Most token-level detectors are trained and evaluated exactly this way: Liu et al. [7] framed tokenlevel reference-free hallucination detection as benchmark task, and corpora such as RAGTruth [10] now support it. The work closest to our concern already hints at why reaction time is the right lens. Snel et al. [17] find that the first token of hallucination span is far more detectable than its continuation tokens, an AUC near 0.8 against near 0.5, which is precisely change-point statement: the onset carries the signal. The same localization question is emerging at coarser granularity, where Alvarez and Baheri [2] locate the first error in reasoning chain as an excursion in hidden-state geometry, and the operational case for reacting during generation is made by Obeso et al. [11], who flag hallucinated entities in real time. None of these poses the streaming problem with an explicit false-alarmdelay trade-off, which is what we add. 1 That trade-off has mature theory. Quickest change detection asks exactly the streaming question: observations switch from one distribution to another at an unknown time, and stopping rule must declare the change as fast as possible while rarely crying wolf [12, 8]. It comes with lower bounds on detection delay that no detector can beat [8, 6], attained asymptotically by Pages CUSUM [9, 13], with the modern state of the field surveyed by Xie et al. [19]. This machinery is standard in quality control, sensor networks, and finance, but to our knowledge it has not been connected to hallucination detection, where the analogous question, how long generation hallucinates before monitor reacts, is the operational one. Xie [20] recently argued that sequential alarms and change-point detection should become standard tools for monitoring deployed LLMs, including shifts in hallucination rates across queries; we supply concrete instance one level down, inside single generation, where the change point is the onset of hallucinated span. When the preand post-change densities are unknown or high-dimensional, learned statistic replaces the fixed log-likelihood ratio: Gong et al. [5] show that the cross-entropy minimizer of such neural CUSUM recovers the log-likelihood ratio, the result we use to read causal recurrent labeler as learned CUSUM. We treat the onset of hallucination as change-point and ask three questions: RQ1 What is the smallest detection delay achievable for hallucination onset at fixed false-alarm rate? RQ2 How close do practical detectors come, and does temporal, learned structure help over per-token scoring? RQ3 If gap to the bound remains, where does it come from? Answering RQ1 needs model of how the hidden faithful/hallucinated state moves. We show it is first-order Markov chain: fitting higher orders is statistically significant but adds under 0.35% of log-likelihood each, so order one captures 99.7% of the structure. That assumption places the task inside Lordens minimax framework and gives floor on delay of about 1.3 tokens at 1% false-alarm rate. For RQ2 we compare detectors at matched false-alarm budget. parametric CUSUM that fits Gaussian densities to the feature stream is far off the floor, at 41 tokens, because diagonal Gaussian is the wrong likelihood model in 33 dimensions. causal recurrent labeler does much better, and we argue it is learned CUSUM: its recurrent state accumulates evidence and its log-odds stand in for the cumulative log-likelihood ratio, with the score function learned rather than assumed. The detector and its 33-dimensional feature stream come from prior work on temporal multi-signal hallucination detection; the closest architectural neighbor reads generations log-probabilities as time series with recurrent network [14], though at the response level and from single signal. Reading the labeler causally presumes hallucination propagates forward through autoregressive conditioning, for which Akarlar [1] give causal support: injecting hallucinated state corrupts the continuation far more often than the reverse repair restores it. At the same false-alarm rate the learned CUSUM detects in 1113 tokens among the onsets it catches, against 31 for the linear per-token baseline. Yet controlled decomposition tempers the temporal reading: nonlinear per-token model with no sequence already reaches 18 tokens, so most of the advantage over the linear baseline is better score; the sequential accumulation contributes about quarter of the reduction (significant under bootstrap) and the extra causal context is within noise. For RQ3, large gap to the 1.3-token floor remains, and first-order rate lets us say where most of it comes from. It is not the architecture. The delay of any score-based detector is set by the 2 information rate its score realizes, and the learned score realizes only 1/4.5 of the divergence the features contain; that shortfall is invariant to recalibration and close to irreducible for these features. further factor of two is finite-horizon: the score is so smoothed in time that the asymptotic correlation penalty overshoots tenfold, and detection happens faster than the score mixes. The gap is feature-discriminability problem first and finite-horizon question second, not depth problem. Low-false-alarm onset detection is also hard in way the bound does not capture: at the floors operating point recall is near 30%, so most onsets go uncaught at their first token, consistent with the difficulty Snel et al. [17] report. Our contributions: 1. We formalize hallucination onset detection as sequential change-point detection and validate the first-order Markov structure the formulation rests on (Section 2, 3.1). 2. We establish Lordens minimax delay bound for the task and compute it from the feature divergence (Section 3.2). 3. We show causal recurrent labeler is learned CUSUM and, with nonlinear per-token baseline, decompose its speedup over linear detector into better score (most of it), sequential accumulation, and context, at matched false-alarm rate (Section 3.3, 4). 4. We give first-order delay rate for any score-based detector (Proposition 1) and use it to attribute the order-of-magnitude gap to 4.5 information-rate shortfall (invariant to recalibration) and finite-horizon residual, showing the asymptotic correlation correction overshoots because detection precedes mixing (Sections 4, 4.3).

sections.title: 2 Hallucination Onset as Sequential Change-Point Detection
sections.content: A generation is token sequence x1, . . . , xT . Each token carries latent faithfulness state Zt {0, 1}, where Zt = 1 marks hallucinated token, and human annotator supplies the labels yt {0, 1} we treat as ground truth. hallucination span is maximal run of consecutive yt = 1. The quantity streaming monitor cares about is not whether token is hallucinated in isolation but when the first one arrives. Definition 1 (Onset). The onset of generation with at least one hallucinated token is θ = min{t : yt = 1}, the start of its first span. generation with no hallucination has θ = . An online detector reads features causally. At step it has seen X1:t = (X1, . . . , Xt), where Xt is the feature vector extracted for token (text statistics, NLI signals, generator log-probabilities), and it must decide whether to raise an alarm using only the past. Formally the detector is stopping time τ with respect to the filtration Ft = σ(X1:t): the event {τ t} is determined by X1:t alone. This is the standard quickest-change-detection setup [19]. Before the onset, features are drawn from pre-change law P0 (the distribution of faithful-token features); from the onset onward they follow post-change law P1 (hallucinated-token features). good detector fires soon after θ without firing before it. The two errors trade off, and the trade-off is measured by two quantities. Definition 2 (Operating characteristics). For stopping rule τ , ARL0(τ ) = E[τ ] and EDD(τ ) = Eθ (cid:2)(τ θ)+(cid:3), where is taken under the no-change law (every token faithful) and Eθ under change at θ. The average run length to false alarm ARL0 counts the mean number of faithful tokens detector 3 Order Log-likelihood Parameters vs. order k1 1 2 3 12,128 12,088 12,052 12,014 2 4 8 16 +0.33% +0.30% +0.32% Table 1: Markov-order selection on hallucination labels. Higher orders are significant by the likelihood-ratio test but add under 0.35% of log-likelihood each. Order one is sufficient. survives before spurious alarm; the expected detection delay EDD counts tokens between onset and alarm. We estimate ARL0 on the stream of faithful tokens (concatenating the hallucination-free generations and counting alarms), so it is not capped by the length of any single document (Appendix B). This matters. tempting alternative is per-document false-alarm rate, the fraction of clean generations that trigger at least once. Yet generation has 120 tokens and therefore independent chances to misfire, so per-document rate of 0.01 demands per-token rate near 104, about times stricter than the per-step rate the theory below controls. Reporting against ARL0 keeps the empirical operating point in the same units as the bound: ARL0 = γ corresponds to per-step false-alarm rate α = 1/γ, and γ = 100 is the canonical α = 0.01. The detectors objective, then, is to minimize EDD subject to ARL0 γ. Section 3 gives the lowest delay any detector can achieve under that constraint, and the structural assumption that makes the bound computable. Section 4 measures how close real detectors get.

sections.title: 3.1 A first-order Markov chain is the right model for the label process
sections.content: The change-point formulation needs model for how the latent state evolves. We model the label sequence {yt} as Markov chain and ask what order is warranted. Fitting orders 1 through 4 on the training labels and comparing by likelihood-ratio test gives Table 1. Every higher order is statistically significant (p < 103, the test has enormous power at this sample size) and practically negligible: each added order lifts the log-likelihood by less than 0.35%. first-order chain captures 99.7% of the attainable sequential structure. Assumption 1 (First-order label dynamics). The label process is first-order Markov chain with transition matrix = (cid:19) (cid:18)1 1 , = P(yt=1 yt1=0), = P(yt=1 yt1=1). On our data = 0.004 and = 0.907. We fit and by maximum likelihood from the annotated labels: every adjacent pair (yt1, yt) over the training generations is counted into 22 table whose rows are then normalized. So is the fraction of faithful tokens immediately followed by hallucinated one (the per-token onset hazard) and the fraction of hallucinated tokens followed by another (persistence); the higher orders of Table 1 use the same count-and-normalize estimator with longer contexts. The rates are 4 displayed to three decimals, so the persistence ratio q/p > 200 quoted below reflects the unrounded 0.0044 rather than 0.907/0.004; Appendix gives the split sizes and the count table. Two consequences matter. The onset hazard is small, so onsets are rare and single changepoint per generation is the right picture. The persistence is large, so once the chain enters the hallucinated state it tends to stay: spans are geometric with mean length 1/(1 q) 11 tokens, long enough that the post-change regime is effectively stationary and the asymptotic theory of the next subsection applies. The ratio q/p > 200 is what makes onset genuine change-point rather than i.i.d. noise. It also explains negative result we report elsewhere: self-exciting (Hawkes) model does not beat this two-parameter chain, because the excitation is one-step, not long-range. The chain is not simplification of the dynamics. It is the dynamics.

sections.title: 3.2 The Lorden bound on detection delay
sections.content: With single change from pre-change law P0 to post-change law P1, classical theory gives floor on delay that no causal detector can beat. Theorem 1 (8, 6). Let D(P1P0) be the KullbackLeibler divergence between the postand pre-change feature laws. Then every stopping rule τ with ARL0(τ ) γ satisfies EDD(τ ) ln γ D(P1 P0) (cid:0)1 + o(1)(cid:1) (γ ), and the CUSUM rule of Page [12] attains this floor asymptotically [9]. The bound is intuitive: each post-change observation supplies on average D(P1P0) nats of evidence, false-alarm budget γ requires accumulating ln γ nats before stopping, so the fastest possible detector needs ln γ/D(P1P0) observations. Estimating the feature divergence on our 33-dimensional signal under diagonal-Gaussian model gives D(P1P0) 3.5 nats, hence EDDmin(γ = 100) = ln 100 3.5 1.3 tokens at α = 0.01. (1) An oracle that observed the labels themselves would do even better: the label-space divergence is 4.6 nats (a floor of 1.0 token), and because = 0.907 makes the post-change label nearly deterministic, such an oracle detects essentially at the onset. The gap between 1.3 tokens (best possible from features) and what real feature detectors achieve is the subject of Section 4. Remark 1. Equation (1) is per-step statement: it controls the false-alarm rate per token, and γ is the mean number of tokens between false alarms. This is why we report ARL0 rather than per-document rate (Section 2); the two differ by factor of the document length.

sections.title: 3.3 A causal recurrent labeler is a learned CUSUM
sections.content: CUSUM, the rule that attains the bound, accumulates the log-likelihood ratio and resets at zero: (cid:16) St = max 0, St1 + log p1(Xt) p0(Xt) (cid:17) , τ = min{t : St h}. (2) It needs the two densities p0, p1. When they are misspecified (as diagonal Gaussian is for 33-dimensional feature stream), the increment is the wrong score and St accumulates noise instead of signal. causal (forward) recurrent labeler replaces the fixed log-likelihood ratio with learned one. It maintains hidden state ht = fϕ(ht1, Xt) and emits posterior ˆpt = σ(wht), so its log-odds logit ˆpt are learned, nonlinear, accumulated statistic of the whole causal history X1:t. To make the correspondence with (2) precise, view the generation as two-state hidden Markov model: the latent state Zt follows the chain of Assumption 1, and the feature vector is drawn from state-conditional emission Xt Zt = pz. Assumption 2 (Emission regularity). The emissions p0, p1 have bounded, Lipschitz densities on the feature space, with finite divergence D(p1 p0). Corollary 1 (Optimal onset detection is thresholded filter, realizable by recurrent network). Under Assumptions 12: (i) (Optimality.) The Bayes-optimal onset detector that minimizes expected delay at fixed falsealarm level is threshold rule on the change posterior πt = P(θ X1:t), finite-dimensional filter that obeys forward recursion on the two-state belief [15, 4]. (ii) (Score.) The minimizer of the per-token cross-entropy loss is the exact log-likelihood ratio φ(x) = log(cid:0)p1(x)/p0(x)(cid:1) [5], which is the CUSUM increment of (2). (iii) (Realizability.) For every ε > 0 there is causal recurrent network whose output approximates the filter in (i) within ε, uniformly in [3]. Chaining (i)(iii): causal recurrent labeler trained by cross-entropy is consistent estimator of the optimal sequential detector. As its approximation error (iii) and the finite-sample error of its score (ii) vanish, the ARL0 and EDD of its thresholded output approach those of the optimal CUSUM, whose delay is the floor of Theorem 1. We state this as corollary because it assembles three existing results (Shiryaev optimality for hidden Markov models, the cross-entropy/log-likelihood-ratio identity, and universal filter approximation) into statement about hallucination onset, whose hypotheses Assumptions 12 verify; the work is in checking the hypotheses, not in new proof. It is limit statement. To turn it into rate, read the detector off the posterior in two ways: threshold ˆpt directly, or feed it through the explicit accumulation (cid:16) St = max 0, St1 + logit ˆpt (cid:17) , = 1 2 (cid:0)µ0 + µ1 (cid:1), (3) where µ0, µ1 are the mean log-odds the model assigns to faithful and hallucinated tokens. The reference value is the textbook CUSUM choice that centers faithful token below zero and hallucinated token above it, whatever the raw posteriors calibration. Proposition 1 (First-order delay of general-score CUSUM). Run (3) on increments Yt = logit ˆpt with E0[Y ] < 0 < E1[Y ], and let ω > 0 solve E0[eωY ] = 1. As the threshold grows, ARL0 = eωh(1 + o(1)) and EDD = h/E1[Y ] (1 + o(1)) [16], so EDD ln ARL0 I(ˆg) , I(ˆg) = ω E1[Y ] is governed by the realized information rate I(ˆg) of the score (proof in Appendix F). The true log-likelihood ratio gives ω = 1 and = D(p1 p0), recovering Theorem 1; for any other score I(ˆg) D(p1 p0) by Theorem 2 below, so the multiplicative gap to the floor is exactly D(p1 p0) /I(ˆg). That the log-likelihood-ratio score is delay-optimal is classical [9]; what we add is the variational form of the shortfall. Writing the rate of general score through the Lundberg exponent makes I(s) coincide with DonskerVaradhan functional, so the gap D/I(s) is exactly the scores Donsker Varadhan deficit. This is the one place we give self-contained proof, and it is what ties the floor to quantity we can measure on the learned score (Section 4). 6 Theorem 2 (Information-rate optimality). Let be any bounded score with increment = s(X) obeying E0[Y ] < 0 < E1[Y ], and let ω > 0 solve E0[eωY ] = 1. Then the realized information rate of Proposition 1 satisfies I(s) = ω E1[Y ] D(p1 p0) , with equality if and only if is an affine function of the log-likelihood ratio log(p1/p0) (P0-almost everywhere). Hence the log-likelihood-ratio score is delay-optimal and attains the floor of Theorem 1; any other scores delay exceeds the floor by the factor D(p1 p0) /I(s). The proof (Appendix F) applies the DonskerVaradhan formula to = ωY , where the Lundberg normalization E0[eωY ] = 1 removes the log-moment term. Proposition 1 and Theorem 2 turn gap remains into number we can read off the data, and isolate the leading cause. Two finite-sample effects hold trained labeler back, and Section 4 measures both. Remark 2 (Where the gap comes from). The dominant factor is the realized divergence: the learned score realizes I(ˆg) < D(p1 p0), and Proposition 1 turns that shortfall directly into delay; Gong et al. [5] reach the same conclusion through maximum-mean-discrepancy surrogate looser than the KL rate. This shortfall is property of the scores shape, not its scale, and Section 4.3 shows it is close to irreducible for our features. smaller residual is finite-horizon: the score increments are strongly correlated, yet detection is faster than the score mixes, so the asymptotic dependent-data rate overshoots rather than tightens the prediction (Section 4.3). This reframes our earlier finding, that recurrent model beats linear per-token classifier by wide margin, as statement about the score function of Corollary 1(ii). The next section measures, at matched false-alarm rate, how much that buys, how much of it is the sequential accumulation as opposed to better per-token score, and how far it still sits from the floor.

sections.title: 4.1 Setup
sections.content: We evaluate on the RAGTruth test split [10]: 2,700 generations, 943 of them containing at least one hallucination, 1,757 clean. Features are the 33-dimensional per-token signal of our base system (text statistics, NLI, generator log-probabilities). Five causal detectors, matched at common ARL0 by sweeping their thresholds: Naive Gaussian CUSUM: the parametric rule (2) with p0, p1 fit as diagonal Gaussians on the feature stream. The misspecified baseline. LogReg (per-token): logistic regression posterior thresholded token by token. Linear, no accumulation. HistGBM (per-token): gradient-boosted per-token classifier on the same features. Nonlinear but still per-token (no accumulation); it isolates how much of recurrent models edge is better score rather than the sequence. ForwardGRU (threshold): forward recurrent labeler whose posterior is thresholded directly. Its recurrent state already accumulates, so this is the learned CUSUM read off without an explicit sum. ForwardGRU (CUSUM): the same posterior fed through the explicit accumulation (3). 7 Detector ARL0 = 50 ARL0 = 100 ARL0 = 200 Delay among detected, tokens (recall) LogReg (linear per-token) HistGBM (nonlinear per-token) Naive Gaussian CUSUM ForwardGRU-shuffled (CUSUM) ForwardGRU (threshold) ForwardGRU (CUSUM) 30.7 (0.35) 14.4 (0.38) 35.9 (0.20) 21.4 (0.33) 30.8 (0.31) 17.9 (0.40) 41 (misspecified, off-scale) 15.6 (0.21) 13.4 (0.30) 11.5 (0.24) 15.2 (0.29) 14.0 (0.27) 13.3 (0.32) 15.3 (0.17) 11.2 (0.26) 9.0 (0.19) Lorden bound (features) Oracle (observes labels) 0.9 1.3 0 (detects at onset) 1.2 Table 2: Detection delay at matched false-alarm budgets. nonlinear per-token model (HistGBM) already closes most of the gap between the linear baseline and the recurrent CUSUM, so the recurrent models edge is mostly better score, not the sequence (decomposition in Figure 1). All stay an order of magnitude above the floor; recall near 30% reflects the difficulty of low-false-alarm onset detection. We report two delays. Delay among detected is the mean of τ θ over generations the detector catches: its speed when it fires. Censored EDD averages over all hallucination generations, charging miss the maximum possible delay (tokens remaining after the onset); it cannot be inflated by low recall. We give recall alongside both.

sections.title: 4.2 Results
sections.content: Table 2 and Figure 1 give the picture at ARL0 = 100 (the α = 0.01 operating point of the bound). Most of the recurrent models speedup over the linear baseline is better per-token score, not the sequence. At ARL0 = 100 the ForwardGRU CUSUM detects in 11.5 tokens against 30.8 for the linear per-token baseline, 2.7 speedup that is tempting to credit to the temporal model. Yet nonlinear per-token classifier with no sequence at all (HistGBM) already detects in 17.9 tokens, covering most of that ground. Decomposing the 30.8 11.5 reduction (Figure 1 and Appendix C; brackets are 95% bootstrap CIs over documents): the nonlinear score accounts for 12.9 tokens [8.8, 17.0] (LogReg to HistGBM) and the CUSUM accumulation for 4.5 [1.8, 7.1] (HistGBM threshold to CUSUM), both significant; the further causal context, 1.9 [1.0, 4.7] (HistGBM-CUSUM to ForwardGRU-CUSUM), is within noise. About two-thirds of the advantage over linear detector is better per-token score; the sequential accumulation that Corollary 1 formalizes is real but modest. The accumulation helps the linear and the nonlinear score about equally (4.5 tokens each), which marks it as genuine, if secondary, effect rather than an artifact of the score. same-architecture control sharpens the point: ForwardGRU trained on token-shuffled sequences (same model, no temporal order) detects in 15.6 tokens, so order is worth about 4 tokens to the recurrent model itself, yet nonlinear per-token model with no sequence (HistGBM, 13.4) already matches the full recurrent CUSUM (11.5). Order helps the network, but it is not needed to reach its performance. The parametric baseline fails for specific, nameable reason. diagonal Gaussian is poor model of the 33-dimensional feature law, so the log-likelihood increment in (2) is the wrong score and the statistic accumulates noise. Its 41-token delay is worse than even the linear posterior. Adding more features makes it worse, not better, because each extra dimension adds Gaussian-model error. 8 Figure 1: Where the speedup comes from, at ARL0 = 100. From the linear per-token baseline (30.8) to the recurrent CUSUM (11.5), most of the reduction is the nonlinear per-token score (12.9, significant); the sequential accumulation (4.5, significant) and causal context (1.9, within noise) are smaller. Error bars are 95% bootstrap CIs over documents. The naive Gaussian CUSUM (dashed) is off-scale; all detectors sit far above the Lorden floor (dotted). The bound is reachable in principle, but only with score function close to the true log(p1/p0), which is exactly what the recurrent model learns and the Gaussian does not. Most of the remaining gap is score-shape shortfall. The ForwardGRU CUSUM detects in 11.5 tokens, about 9 the 1.3-token floor. Proposition 1 locates most of it. The learned score realizes an information rate I(ˆg) = ω δ1 = 0.78 nats per token (ω = 0.95, δ1 = 0.82), well below the 3.5 nats of the feature divergence, so its i.i.d. first-order delay is ln(100)/I(ˆg) = 5.9 tokens, factor of D/I(ˆg) = 4.5 above the floor. The score is nearly valid likelihood ratio (ω 1); what it lacks is post-change drift. This 4.5 is deficit relative to the i.i.d. first-order rate of Proposition 1, not of the true gap: the rates i.i.d. hypothesis is violated by the learned score (4.3 shows its increments are strongly correlated). So the decomposition that follows is factorization against that idealization. The remaining factor of about 2, from 5.9 predicted to 11.5 observed, is exactly where the i.i.d. rate breaks; we examine both next.

sections.title: 4.3 Closing the gap: what moves the rate, and what does not
sections.content: Rescaling the score does not move its rate, and reshaping it barely does. Temperature scaling (cid:55) s/T leaves I(ˆg) exactly unchanged (ω (cid:55) ωT , δ1 (cid:55) δ1/T ): the shortfall is in the shape of the score, not its calibrated scale, so recalibrating confidence cannot help. monotone-nonlinear reshaping (isotonic regression of the posterior to the labels) does change the shape, yet recovers only +12% of I(ˆg) (0.78 0.87). The 4.5 shortfall is close to irreducible for these features: the per-token signal does not separate faithful from hallucinated tokens as sharply as the marginal feature divergence suggests. Shrinking it needs more discriminative features, not retuned model. The residual factor of two is finite-horizon, not an asymptotic correlation effect. The learned its clean-stream autocorrelation decays slowly (ρ1 = 0.94), score is strongly smoothed in time: 9 with integrated autocorrelation time τ 22. Correlated increments should, asymptotically, deflate the rate, and indeed the adjustment coefficient read from the detectors own ARL0threshold curve is ω 0.044, below the marginal ω = 0.95 by precisely the factor τ . Yet the asymptotic dependent-data rate [6] then predicts delay near 126 tokens, an order of magnitude past what we observe. It overshoots because detection here is faster than mixing: the onset is caught in 11 tokens, well inside the scores τ 22 correlation time, and the reset CUSUM floors ARL0 at the mean document length. Neither limit is exact in this regime. The i.i.d. rate is lower bound on delay (5.9); the realized 11.5 sits factor of 2 above it, and pinning that down is finite-horizon question, not an asymptotic correlation correction. Low-false-alarm onset detection is intrinsically hard. Recall is near 30% at ARL0 = 100 for every realistic detector, so the recall-honest censored EDD is 5666 tokens across the board: most onsets are simply not caught within tight false-alarm budget. This is consistent with Snel et al. [17], who find the first hallucinated token detectable at an AUC around 0.8 rather than 1.0. The first token of span is the most detectable one, but it is far from trivially detectable, and streaming monitor that must hold its false-alarm rate down will miss most onsets at their first token.

sections.title: 5 Discussion
sections.content: Treating hallucination onset as change-point buys something the classification view lacks: yardstick. Token AUC rewards getting the average token right and says nothing about reaction time, whereas the Lorden floor states, in the units deployment cares about, how fast any detector could possibly be. Read against that floor, our measurements carry two lessons. The first is that the temporal frame is the right one but is not where the empirical win comes from. causal recurrent labeler is learned CUSUM, and the learning matters: it detects two to three times faster than parametric CUSUM or linear per-token model at matched false-alarm rate. What the learning buys, though, is mostly better per-token score. nonlinear per-token classifier with no sequence already closes most of the gap to the recurrent CUSUM, leaving the sequential accumulation as real but secondary effect and the extra causal context within bootstrap noise. We read this as clarification of temporal modeling rather than demotion of it: the sequential-detection framework is what makes the delay measurable and gives the recurrent labeler its CUSUM interpretation, while the magnitude of the empirical speedup is set by score quality. The second lesson concerns the order-of-magnitude gap that remains between the best detector and the floor, and it is not an argument for deeper models. The delay of any score-based detector is fixed by the information rate its score realizes, and our learned score realizes only about fifth of the divergence the features carry, deficit that is invariant to recalibration and barely moves under monotone reshaping. The bound is property of the information in the features, so the way to halve the achievable delay is to extract features that separate the two regimes more sharply, not to add depth. This is general statement about score-based onset detection, not quirk of our architecture. third point is easy to miss behind an AUC and worth stating plainly. At the floors operating point our detectors catch under third of onsets at their first token. Low-false-alarm streaming detection is genuinely hard: monitor that promises to flag hallucinations as they happen, without burying the user in false alarms, will miss most onsets at the first opportunity and catch them span later, if at all. That is the regime real deployments live in, and token-level AUC hides it. Because the detector needs no access to the generating model, it remains applicable to closed-source APIs where white-box probes do not; the sequential view simply makes its true operating cost visible.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: Hallucination onset is change-point, and treating it as one buys yardstick the classification view lacks: hard floor on how fast any detector can react. For RAGTruth that floor is about 1.3 tokens at 1% false-alarm rate, and it rests on fact worth stating plainly, that the faithful/hallucinated state is first-order Markov chain and nothing more elaborate is needed. Against that floor, causal recurrent labeler acts as learned CUSUM and detects, among the onsets it catches, two to three times faster than parametric CUSUM or linear per-token baseline, though controlled decomposition shows most of that advantage is better per-token score rather than the sequence, and the detector still sits an order of magnitude above the bound. Two directions follow, and the analysis singles out the first as dominant. The larger lever is the divergence the score can realize: feature set that doubles D(P1P0) halves the achievable delay, and unlike recalibration it actually moves the rate. The second is theoretical. The factor-of-two residual lives in regime where detection is faster than the score mixes, so neither the i.i.d. first-order rate nor the asymptotic correlation correction is tight; finite-horizon analysis of CUSUM delay under strongly autocorrelated score would close it. Both are concrete, and both are measurable against the bound this paper puts on the table.

sections.title: 7 Limitations
sections.content: The bound and the theorem rest on idealizations worth stating plainly, because each one bears on how the numbers should be read. The first is that the emissions are not i.i.d. given the state. Theorem 1 and the divergence in (1) treat the per-token features as independent draws from P0 or P1 conditional on the state, yet several of our 33 features are windowed or cumulative, so consecutive tokens are correlated even within one regime. Correlated observations carry less information per token than i.i.d. ones, so the true per-token evidence is below D(P1P0) and the floor of 1.3 tokens is, if anything, optimistic; the gap we report is therefore lower bound on the true gap. The divergence itself is only diagonal-Gaussian estimate. We compute D(P1P0) 3.5 nats under diagonal-Gaussian model of the feature law, which ignores cross-feature dependence and non-Gaussian shape, and different estimator would move the floor. We use this model because the naive CUSUM baseline assumes it too, which keeps that comparison fair, but the precise value of the bound should be read as an order of magnitude rather than constant to three digits. As check, non-parametric k-nearest-neighbour estimator [18] of the same divergence gives 2.8 nats (floor 1.6 tokens), in line with the diagonal-Gaussian value, so the floor is robust to the estimator. The delay rate is first-order, and its residual is measured rather than bounded. Proposition 1 expresses the delay through the realized information rate I(ˆg), but it is first-order in the threshold and treats the score increments as i.i.d. Section 4.3 shows the leftover factor of two is finite-horizon: the asymptotic correlation correction overshoots by an order of magnitude because detection precedes the scores mixing time, so neither limit is tight. We measure that residual rather than bound it; finite-horizon rate for CUSUM under strongly autocorrelated score would be stronger, and harder. Our analysis also covers only the first onset of each generation. The optimality in Corollary 1(i) is for single change-point, so generations with several spans, and the question of re-arming the detector after span ends, are out of scope; the first-passage view matches deployment that stops at the first alarm rather than one that monitors continuously through whole generation. Finally, the numbers come from one corpus and one operating regime. The transition probabilities p, q, the divergence, and hence the bound are estimated on RAGTruth, so while the qualitative ordering of detectors should transfer, the specific values, and the ARL0 at which recall collapses, are corpus-specific. The delay numbers come from trained model (seed 42); we quantify document-level uncertainty with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (Figure 1) and additionally seed-average the decomposition over five seeds (Appendix C), where the accumulation step stays robust (3.4 0.7 tokens) and the causal-context step stays within noise, confirming the single-seed reading.

sections.title: A Data and Label Estimation
sections.content: We use RAGTruth [10] with its official train/test split. The test split has 2,700 generations (943 with at least one hallucinated span, 1,757 clean) and about 341,000 tokens at 4.16% hallucination rate; the training split, on which the transition probabilities and the feature divergence are estimated, holds about 1.98 million tokens. token is labeled 1 when it falls inside human-annotated hallucination span and 0 otherwise. The transition matrix is the maximum-likelihood estimate of two-state chain. Every adjacent label pair (yt1, yt) over the training generations is counted into 2 2 table C, and each row is normalized: = C01 C00 + C01 , = C11 C10 + C11 . This gives the onset hazard 0.0044 and the persistence 0.907, ratio q/p > 200. The higher-order rows of Table 1 use the same count-and-normalize estimator with length-k context. The labels carried by the saved detector posteriors match the reference RAGTruth labels up to relabeling difference of about 0.05% of tokens, too small to move p, q, or the bound. Operating-Characteristic Estimation We estimate ARL0 on the concatenation of all hallucination-free generations. The detector is run over this clean stream at fixed threshold, and ARL0 is the total number of clean tokens divided by the number of distinct alarms, where an alarm is counted at each upward threshold crossing and the statistic is reset afterward. Because the stream is not segmented by document, ARL0 is not capped by document length, unlike per-document false-alarm rate. Delay is measured on the hallucination generations: the alarm position minus the onset θ when the detector fires at or after θ, and the maximum possible delay (tokens remaining after θ) when it misses or fires early, the latter giving the censored EDD. Detectors are matched by sweeping their thresholds to common ARL0 before any delay is compared.

sections.title: C Speedup Decomposition and Bootstrap
sections.content: The decomposition of Figure 1 fixes each detectors threshold once on the full data at the ARL0 = 100 operating point, then resamples the hallucination generations with replacement (B = 1,000) and recomputes the delay among detected at those fixed thresholds. This conditional bootstrap captures document-level variance without re-matching ARL0 on every resample. The three steps are the nonlinear score (LogReg to HistGBM, 12.9 tokens, 95% confidence interval [8.8, 17.0]), the accumulation (HistGBM threshold to CUSUM, 4.5, [1.8, 7.1]), and the causal context (HistGBMCUSUM to ForwardGRU-CUSUM, 1.9, [1.0, 4.7]); the first two intervals exclude zero and the third does not. ForwardGRU trained on token-shuffled sequences (15.6 tokens) isolates the value of temporal order to the recurrent model itself. The bootstrap above varies documents at fixed model; we also seed-average the decomposition by retraining all detectors under five seeds (0, 1, 2, 7, 42). The two recurrent-model-dependent steps survive training variance: the accumulation reduces the delay by 3.4 0.7 tokens (robust, more than one standard deviation from zero), the causal-context step is 0.9 1.6 tokens (within one standard deviation of zero), and the ForwardGRU-CUSUM delay is 12.2 1.4 tokens. The document-variance 14 picture therefore carries over to training variance: the sequential accumulation is real effect and the extra causal context is not.

sections.title: D Models and Features
sections.content: All detectors share the same 33-dimensional per-token feature vector (text statistics, NLI entailment, and generator log-probabilities) from the companion multi-signal system. The ForwardGRU is two-layer unidirectional GRU of hidden width 64 with sigmoid output head, trained by binary cross-entropy with AdamW (weight decay 104) under seed 42 and 15% stratified validation split. The HistGBM is gradient-boosted classifier (500 trees, learning rate 0.05, up to 63 leaves per tree) on the same features, and the linear baseline is class-balanced logistic regression. The full training recipe and the feature extractor are released with the companion system. Closing the Gap: Details The realized information rate of the learned score is I(ˆg) = ω δ1 = 0.78 nats per token, with Lundberg exponent ω = 0.95 and post-change drift δ1 = 0.82, against feature divergence 3.5 nats, so D/I(ˆg) = 4.5. Temperature scaling (cid:55) s/T leaves I(ˆg) exactly invariant (ω (cid:55) ωT , δ1 (cid:55) δ1/T ); isotonic recalibration of the posterior to the labels changes the scores shape but recovers only +12% (0.78 0.87). On the clean stream the score is strongly autocorrelated (ρ1 = 0.94, integrated autocorrelation time τ 22), and the adjustment coefficient read from the detectors own ARL0threshold curve, ω 0.044, falls below the marginal ω by the factor τ . The asymptotic dependent-data rate then predicts delay near 126 tokens, an order of magnitude past the observed 11.5, because detection occurs well inside the scores correlation time; this is the finite-horizon residual of Section 4.3.

sections.title: F Proofs
sections.content: Throughout, Yt = s(Xt) are the centered score increments, the CUSUM is St = max(0, St1 + Yt), and τ = inf{t : St h} is its stopping time. Write δ1 = E1[Y ] > 0 for the post-change drift and let ω > 0 be the Lundberg exponent solving E0[eωY ] = 1. Proposition 1 (first-order delay). Delay. After the change the increments have positive mean δ1, so St is random walk with drift δ1 reflected at 0. Walds identity gives E1[Sτ ] = δ1 E1[τ ]. Neglecting the overshoot Sτ (bounded in expectation under mild integrability condition on ), E1[Sτ ] = h(1 + o(1)), hence EDD = E1[τ ] = δ1 (1 + o(1)). False alarm. Under P0 the increments have negative mean, so the walk drifts down and false alarm is large deviation. Tilt by the Lundberg exponent, P0 = eωY dP0; by E0[eωY ] = 1 this is probability law under which the walk has positive drift, and the standard renewal estimate for the level-crossing of tilted walk [16] gives ARL0 = E[τ ] = eωh(1+o(1)), i.e. = ω1 ln ARL0 (1+o(1)). Combining, EDD = δ1 ln ARL0 ω δ1 = ln ARL0 I(s) , I(s) = ω δ1. 15 Theorem 2 (information-rate optimality). Write = D(p1 p0). The DonskerVaradhan variational formula states that for every measurable with E0[ef ] < , E1[f ] log (cid:2)ef (cid:3) D, (4) with equality if and only if ef dP1/dP0 (P0-a.e.). Apply (4) with = ωY . The Lundberg condition E0[eωY ] = 1 makes the second term vanish, so I(s) = ω E1[Y ] = E1[ωY ] log E0 (cid:2)eωY (cid:3) D. Equality holds iff eωY dP1/dP0, i.e. ωY = log(p1/p0) + for constant c, i.e. is an affine function of the log-likelihood ratio. For the delay statement, Proposition 1 gives EDD ln(ARL0)/I(s) ln(ARL0)/D, with the lower bound attained by the log-likelihood-ratio score, which is exactly the floor of Theorem 1. Remark. The two results compose into the gap identity used in Section 4: at false-alarm budget ARL0, the delay of score is ln(ARL0)/I(s) and the floor is ln(ARL0)/D, so the multiplicative gap is D/I(s) 1, the DonskerVaradhan deficit of the score. The empirical D/I(ˆg) 4.5 for the learned score is this deficit, measured.
